results.possibleIdentity: John C. Parker is a materials scientist and engineer whose career has spanned several decades, specifically focusing on the intersection of materials science, nanotechnology, and art preservation. He has been a frequent contributor and editor for the 'Materials Issues in Art and Archaeology' symposium series (part of the Materials Research Society), which promotes multidisciplinary collaborations between artists, engineers, and scientists—a core goal aligned with that of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His work often explores commercialization opportunities for nanophase materials and the technical analysis of artistic pigments and structures. He was active in research and collaborative publication from the 1970s through the 2000s.

Julie Parker is a British artist and founding member of the collaborative group Action Space, established in 1968. Action Space was a pioneer in the 'art and technology' movement of the late 1960s and early 1970s, known for creating large-scale inflatable structures and participatory performance environments that required close collaboration between artists and engineers. Although based primarily in the United Kingdom, her work with Action Space Mobile and later theatrical ventures (like the Sidewalk Theatre Company) occurred during the same era as the peak activity of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and shared its experimental, interdisciplinary ethos.

Sam Parker (Samuel Murray Parker, born 1936) is an American artist active in the early 1970s. His work, specifically the 1973 color silkscreen titled 'Electric Soup,' is held in major museum collections such as the Art Institute of Chicago. His work is historically categorized alongside pieces from the 'New York Collection for Stockholm' (1973), which was a major publication and donation project organized by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Parker's practice in the late 1960s and 1970s involved silkscreen printing and geometric abstractions, contributing to the broader pop and technical art movements supported by E.A.T. at the time.

results.possibleIdentity: John Ruddley was an art administrator, educator, and scholar active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He served as the Director of the Westchester Art Workshop (WAW) in White Plains, New York, for several years, including the late 1970s. In 1969, he served as a juror for the Westchester Art Society. Ruddley was significantly involved in the administration of arts funding and development in New York, notably appearing as a staff member or consultant for the New York State Council on the Arts (NYSCA) in their 1969-1970 annual report. This role placed him in the same professional circle as the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which received NYSCA grants at that time to facilitate collaborations between artists and engineers. Additionally, Ruddley is recognized for his contributions to major art history scholarship; he provided revisions and additions to prominent textbooks, including Daniel M. Mendelowitz's 'A History of American Art' and David M. Robb's 'The Art of the Illuminated Manuscript.'

results.possibleIdentity: Jean Toche (1932–2018), born Jean Xavier Van Imschoot in Bruges, Belgium, was a Belgian-born American artist, poet, and radical political activist active in New York City from the mid-1960s until his death. Initially an abstract expressionist in the 1950s, Toche moved to New York in 1965 and became a prominent figure in the 'Destruction Art' movement and the Art Workers Coalition (AWC). In October 1969, he co-founded the Guerrilla Art Action Group (GAAG) with Jon Hendricks and Poppy Johnson, a collective known for confrontational, non-violent performances like the 'Blood Bath' (1969) at the Museum of Modern Art, which protested the Vietnam War and institutional corruption. He was famously arrested in 1970 as one of the 'Judson 3' for organizing 'The People's Flag Show' at Judson Memorial Church, and again in 1974 by the FBI for a flyer calling for the 'symbolic' kidnapping of museum officials. Toche was also a member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), with his membership records dating between 1967 and 1969; he specifically collaborated with E.A.T. engineer Per Biorn in 1970. In his later years, he focused on mail art and large-scale photo-text combinations that continued his critique of government policy and nationalism.

results.possibleIdentity: Gary Allen is an artist, photographer, and writer who was a prominent figure in the artistic community of New Paltz, New York, during the 1960s and 1970s. As a student at SUNY New Paltz, he studied under professor Robert Schuler, with whom he collaborated on several experimental projects. Allen was active in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which fostered partnerships between the arts and engineering sectors. His artistic practice included painting, illustration, and postal art, often incorporating scientific and mathematical principles; he notably used mathematics to generate color sequences for his paintings and designed complex lighting systems. Alongside Robert Schuler, he also operated a business focused on constructing experimental buildings using sprayed urethane foam. In his later career, Allen transitioned primarily into writing and food history, authoring or contributing to over 40 books, including 'The Herbalist in the Kitchen' and 'Can It!: The Perils and Pleasures of Preserving Food', while continuing to practice photography.
